issue_title: Is priting observations in callbacks.py is necessary?
issue_body:
If we use a MultiInputEnv like [MultiInputTestEnv](https://github.com/keras-rl/keras-rl/blob/master/tests/rl/util.py), observations could be a list of ndarray. But, in callbacks.py, it prints 'obs_mean','obs_min' and 'obs_max'.
```        
        variables = {
             ...
            'obs_mean': np.mean(self.observations[episode]),
            'obs_min': np.min(self.observations[episode]),
            'obs_max': np.max(self.observations[episode]),
             ... }
        print(template.format(**variables))
```
In [some cases](https://gist.github.com/uenoku/553b88f56322f389a44694f8063809af), it arises errors. If I erase 'obs_*' from variables, error doesn't occur. I think observations mean, min and max are not so important (Perhaps it is wrong because I'm a beginner on RL). Then I'd like to propose that we simply print raw self.observations or don't print it.

issue_title: How to break a big continuous line?
issue_body:
I have this line on my grammar:
```regex
        productions : new_line* ( space* non_terminal_start space* "->" space* non_terminals space* end_symbol )* space* non_terminal_start space* "->" space* non_terminals space* end_symbol?
```
I would like to break it like this:
```regex
        productions : new_line* ( space* non_terminal_start space* "->" space* non_terminals space* end_symbol )* 
                      space* non_terminal_start space* "->" space* non_terminals space* end_symbol?
```
But the lark does not like it when I try to run it:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "F:\Python\lib\site-packages\lark\parsers\lalr_parser.py", line 46, in get_action
    return states[state][key]
KeyError: 'OP'

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):

    from source.grammar import ChomskyGrammar
  File "D:\grammar.py", line 21, in <module>
    class ChomskyGrammar():
  File "D:\grammar.py", line 67, in ChomskyGrammar
    """, start='productions' )
  File "F:\Python\lib\site-packages\lark\lark.py", line 153, in __init__
    self.grammar = load_grammar(grammar, source)
  File "F:\Python\lib\site-packages\lark\load_grammar.py", line 599, in load_grammar
    tree = self.canonize_tree.transform( self.parser.parse(grammar_text+'\n') )
  File "F:\Python\lib\site-packages\lark\parser_frontends.py", line 37, in parse
    return self.parser.parse(token_stream)
  File "F:\Python\lib\site-packages\lark\parsers\lalr_parser.py", line 71, in parse
    action, arg = get_action(token.type)
  File "F:\Python\lib\site-packages\lark\parsers\lalr_parser.py", line 50, in get_action
    raise UnexpectedToken(token, expected, seq, i)
lark.common.UnexpectedToken: Unexpected token Token(OP, '*') at line 3, column 29.
Expected: dict_keys(['_COLON', '_DOT'])
Context: <no context>

I tried to put "\\" (backslash) at the end of the first line, but still the same.

It work fine if I do not break the line and keep it like this:
```regex
        productions : new_line* ( space* non_terminal_start space* "->" space* non_terminals space* end_symbol )* space* non_terminal_start space* "->" space* non_terminals space* end_symbol?
```

issue_body: This is to see how good our tests are and to help everyone adding tests later.

Treat cygwin like linux, because its ps command is limited and the proc file system works.

```
 src/shellingham/posix/__init__.py | 2 +-
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)

@@ -6,7 +6,7 @@ from .._consts import SHELL_NAMES
 
 def _get_process_mapping():
     system = platform.system()
-    if system == 'Linux':

         from . import linux as impl
     else:
         from . import _default as impl

I think the "6.1" string may change a couple times a year. It may be better to do:
```
if system == 'Linux' or system.startswith('CYGWIN_NT'):

issue_title: `drop` does not always drop columns or rows on the same partition
issue_body:
### Describe the problem
<!-- Describe the problem clearly here. -->
`drop` does not drop columns or rows from the same partition if they contain the same name. This required a large refactor of the overall implementation.

### Source code / logs
<!-- Include any logs or source code that would be helpful to diagnose the problem. If including tracebacks, please include the full traceback. Large logs and files should be attached. Try to provide a reproducible test case that is the bare minimum necessary to generate the problem. -->
```python
import modin.pandas as pd
pd.DEFAULT_NPARTITIONS = 2

nu_df = pandas.DataFrame(pandas.compat.lzip(range(3), range(-3, 1),
                                                 list('abc')), columns=['a', 'a', 'b'])
ray_nu_df = pd.DataFrame(nu_df)
x = ray_nu_df.drop('a', axis=1)
y =  nu_df[['b']]
print(x)
```

Printing `x` gives an error because it did not drop all of the columns it should have. However, 

```python
x._col_partitions
print(x)
```

Works correctly.

issue_title: Reader fails on File Resource stream, when file is less than 4KB

issue_body:
Only the basic methods of the socket need to be Cython:

* init
* close
* send
* recv
* getsockopt
* setsockopt

All the derivative and sugar methods (multipart, json, sockopts as attrs, etc.) do not need to be Cython, and do not benefit from being compiled.  I would like to split the Socket file into two: a minimal base class in Cython, and the extended methods on a subclass in Python.

This would reduce compile time, and significantly reduce the need to recompile when working on new features, as the core methods haven't been touched in quite some time.

I think 2.1.11 is pretty good, and the coming 2.1.12 release should be good enough that we don't need to go out of our way to continue to support Python 2.5, whose last bugfix release was over three years ago.  I think the first libzmq-3.1 stable release, which should come some time this year (summer seems the most likely timeframe for stable) would be a good time to drop Python 2.5 support, and cleanup much of the ugly compat code.

When testing the current trunk (2011-12-16), I'm not able to send newsletters:

2011-12-16 23:51:03 INFO Products.EasyNewsletter Sending newsletter to "testuser@gmail.com" failed, with error "MIMEMultipart instance has no attribute 'lstrip'"!

I think this was not happening wiht release 2.6.
